name: Turkey
tagline: From Neolithic Çatalhöyük and the Hittites through Byzantium and the Ottomans to Atatürk's republic.
summary: The land of modern Türkiye carries one of the world's deepest human pasts: the Neolithic town of Çatalhöyük, the Bronze Age Hittite empire centred on Hattusa, and the legendary city of Troy. Greek, Roman and then Byzantine Anatolia made Constantinople a Christian imperial capital for a thousand years, until the Seljuk victory at Manzikert (1071) opened the peninsula to Turkic settlement. From a frontier principality founded around 1299, the Ottoman dynasty rose to take Constantinople in 1453 and to rule a vast empire that peaked under Süleyman the Magnificent. The empire's long decline ended in the catastrophe of the First World War, the Armenian Genocide of 1915–16, and partition — out of which Mustafa Kemal Atatürk forged a national movement that won the War of Independence and proclaimed the Republic of Turkey on 29 October 1923.

founding.label: Proclamation of the Republic of Turkey
founding.detail: Modern Türkiye dates its political founding to 29 October 1923, when the Grand National Assembly in Ankara proclaimed the Republic of Turkey and elected Mustafa Kemal — later surnamed Atatürk, 'Father of the Turks' — as its first president. The republic rose on an Anatolian land of extraordinary depth: the Neolithic settlement of Çatalhöyük (occupied c. 7100–5700 BCE), the Hittite empire at Hattusa, and the city of Troy of Homer's Iliad. Greek and Roman Anatolia gave way to Byzantine Constantinople, the Christian imperial capital refounded by Constantine in 330 CE. After the Seljuk Turks broke Byzantine power at Manzikert in 1071, a frontier emirate founded around 1299 by Osman I grew into the Ottoman Empire, which took Constantinople in 1453 and endured until its collapse after the First World War.

quickFacts.label: Political founding
quickFacts.value: Republic of Turkey proclaimed 29 October 1923 (Atatürk first president)

quickFacts.label: Ottoman Empire
quickFacts.value: Founded c. 1299 (Osman I); took Constantinople 1453; dissolved 1922–23

quickFacts.label: Deep past
quickFacts.value: Çatalhöyük (c. 7100 BCE), Hittites, Troy, Byzantine Constantinople

quickFacts.label: Official name
quickFacts.value: Türkiye, registered at the UN in June 2022

quickFacts.label: Alliances
quickFacts.value: NATO member since 1952; EU candidate since 1999

eras.title: Ancient Anatolia: Çatalhöyük, the Hittites and Troy
eras.standfirst: Anatolia holds some of humanity's earliest towns and one of the great Bronze Age empires, alongside the city immortalised by Homer.
body: Few landscapes preserve as deep a human record as Anatolia. On the Konya plain, the Neolithic settlement of Çatalhöyük was inhabited for some 1,400 years, roughly between 7100 and 5700 BCE. Its densely packed mudbrick houses, entered through the roof, and its wall paintings, reliefs and figurines document the transition from village life to a proto-urban world; the site was inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site in 2012.

In the Bronze Age, central Anatolia became the heartland of the Hittites, an Indo-European people who built an empire from their capital Hattusa (near modern Boğazkale). Hattusilis I made Hattusa his seat in the 17th century BCE, and under rulers such as Suppiluliuma I the Hittites rivalled Egypt, clashing with the pharaohs at the Battle of Kadesh and producing some of the earliest known international treaties.

On the Aegean coast at the mound of Hisarlık stood Troy, the city of Homer's Iliad. Excavations begun by Heinrich Schliemann in 1870 revealed not one city but a sequence of settlements spanning some four thousand years; the level known as Troy VI–VII is the likeliest candidate for the Troy of legend. The Hittite world and the wider eastern Mediterranean were swept away around 1200 BCE in the Bronze Age collapse.

events.title: The Neolithic town of Çatalhöyük
events.summary: On the Konya plain, the streetless settlement of Çatalhöyük flourished for over a millennium (c. 7100–5700 BCE), one of the world's best-preserved Neolithic towns, with roof-access houses and elaborate wall art.

events.title: Hattusa becomes the Hittite capital
events.summary: King Hattusilis I established Hattusa in north-central Anatolia as the capital of the Hittite kingdom, the seat of a Bronze Age empire that would rival Egypt.

events.title: Battle of Kadesh and the Hittite–Egyptian treaty
events.summary: The Hittites under Muwatalli II fought Ramesses II of Egypt at Kadesh; the conflict ended in one of history's earliest surviving peace treaties between the two empires.

events.title: Troy of the Iliad
events.summary: The fortified city at Hisarlık (Troy VI–VII) corresponds to the era of the Trojan War remembered in Homer's Iliad; the layered ruins were excavated from 1870 and inscribed by UNESCO in 1998.

events.title: Bronze Age collapse
events.summary: Around 1200–1180 BCE the Hittite state and Hattusa were destroyed amid the wider collapse of eastern Mediterranean civilisations, ending Anatolia's first great imperial age.

figures.name: Suppiluliuma I
figures.blurb: Expanded the Hittite kingdom into a great power spanning much of Anatolia and the northern Levant, making it a rival of New Kingdom Egypt and Mitanni.

eras.title: Greek, Roman and Byzantine Anatolia
eras.standfirst: Greek colonists, then Roman and Byzantine emperors, made Anatolia a Hellenic and Christian heartland centred on Constantinople.
body: After the Bronze Age collapse, Greek settlers colonised the Aegean coast, founding cities such as Ephesus, Miletus and Smyrna, while inland kingdoms — Phrygia, Lydia and others — rose and fell. Lydia, under kings like Croesus, is credited with minting some of the world's first coins. The region passed under Persian rule before Alexander the Great's conquests spread Hellenistic culture across Anatolia in the late 4th century BCE.

Rome absorbed Anatolia from the 2nd century BCE, and the peninsula — 'Asia Minor' — became one of the wealthiest, most urbanised parts of the empire. It was also a cradle of early Christianity: Paul of Tarsus was an Anatolian, the seven churches of Revelation lay in the west, and the great ecumenical councils of Nicaea (325) and Chalcedon (451) met on Anatolian soil.

In 330 CE Constantine the Great refounded the Greek town of Byzantium as Constantinople, a 'New Rome' guarding the Bosporus. As the western empire fell, the Greek-speaking Christian state of the eastern Romans — the Byzantine Empire — endured for over a thousand years, reaching a high point under Justinian I, who built the Hagia Sophia in the 530s. Repeated wars with Persians and Arabs strained the empire, and in 1071 the Seljuk Turks shattered the Byzantine army at Manzikert, opening Anatolia to Turkic settlement.

events.title: Fall of Lydia and Croesus
events.summary: The wealthy Lydian kingdom of western Anatolia, credited with pioneering coined money, fell to Cyrus the Great of Persia, bringing Anatolia under Persian rule.

events.title: Constantine refounds Constantinople
events.summary: Constantine the Great dedicated the rebuilt city of Byzantium as Constantinople, a new Christian capital on the Bosporus that would anchor the Roman and then Byzantine state for over a millennium.

events.title: Justinian completes the Hagia Sophia
events.summary: Emperor Justinian I inaugurated the great domed church of Hagia Sophia in Constantinople, the masterpiece of Byzantine architecture and for centuries the largest cathedral in the world.

events.title: Battle of Manzikert
events.summary: The Seljuk sultan Alp Arslan defeated and captured the Byzantine emperor Romanos IV at Manzikert, breaking Byzantine control of the interior and opening Anatolia to Turkish settlement.

figures.name: Justinian I
figures.blurb: Reconquered parts of the western Mediterranean, codified Roman law in the Corpus Juris Civilis, and built the Hagia Sophia, the high point of early Byzantine power.

pullquote.text: Constantine dedicated his 'New Rome' on the Bosporus in 330, anchoring a Christian empire that would last more than a thousand years.

eras.title: The Rise of the Seljuks and Ottomans
eras.standfirst: Turkic peoples settled Anatolia, and from a small frontier emirate the Ottoman dynasty rose to take Constantinople.
body: In the wake of Manzikert, Turkic tribes spread across Anatolia. The Seljuks of Rûm established a sultanate centred on Konya, presiding over a flourishing of Islamic art, architecture and learning — the era of the poet and mystic Rumi. The Mongol invasions of the 13th century fragmented Seljuk power into a patchwork of Turkish principalities, or beyliks, competing on the Byzantine frontier.

Out of one such frontier emirate in north-western Anatolia rose the Ottomans. Tradition dates the dynasty's foundation to around 1299, under Osman I, whose name the dynasty bears. His successors expanded rapidly: Bursa fell in 1326 and became an early capital, and by crossing into Europe the Ottomans took Adrianople (Edirne) and won the Battle of Kosovo in 1389, encircling a shrinking Byzantium.

A shattering setback came in 1402, when the conqueror Timur (Tamerlane) defeated and captured Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara, throwing the empire into a civil war known as the Interregnum. The dynasty recovered, and in 1453 the young sultan Mehmed II besieged and stormed Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire and making the city — increasingly called Istanbul — the Ottoman capital.

events.title: Sultanate of Rûm at Konya
events.summary: Seljuk Turks established the Sultanate of Rûm in Anatolia, with Konya as its centre, fostering a rich Islamic culture associated with the mystic poet Rumi.

events.title: Foundation of the Ottoman emirate
events.summary: Osman I, leader of a frontier emirate in north-western Anatolia, is traditionally regarded as founder of the Ottoman dynasty around 1299, giving his name to the empire.

events.title: Battle of Kosovo
events.summary: Ottoman forces under Murad I defeated a Serbian-led coalition at Kosovo, cementing Ottoman dominance in the Balkans even as Murad died on the field.

events.title: Battle of Ankara and the Interregnum
events.summary: Timur (Tamerlane) crushed and captured Sultan Bayezid I at Ankara, plunging the Ottoman state into a decade of civil war before it recovered.

events.title: Fall of Constantinople
events.summary: After a 53-day siege, Sultan Mehmed II captured Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire; the city became the Ottoman capital and Hagia Sophia was converted to a mosque.

figures.name: Osman I
figures.blurb: Leader of a Turkish frontier emirate in north-western Anatolia whose expansion around 1299 marks the traditional founding of the Ottoman state that bears his name.

figures.name: Mehmed II ('the Conqueror')
figures.blurb: Captured Constantinople in 1453 at the age of 21, ending the Byzantine Empire and transforming the Ottoman state into an empire straddling Europe and Asia.

pullquote.text: After a 53-day siege the Byzantine Empire — nearly 1,500 years of Roman continuity — came to an end at Constantinople in 1453.

eras.title: The Ottoman Empire: Height and Decline
eras.standfirst: Under Süleyman the Magnificent the empire reached its zenith, before centuries of contraction earned it the name 'the sick man of Europe'.
body: The 16th century was the Ottoman golden age. Süleyman I, called 'the Magnificent' in Europe and 'the Lawgiver' (Kanuni) at home, reigned from 1520 to 1566 over an empire spanning the Middle East, North Africa and south-eastern Europe. His armies took Belgrade and Rhodes, won at Mohács in 1526, and besieged Vienna in 1529; his court patronised a flourishing of architecture under the master builder Sinan and codified Ottoman law.

The empire remained a great power into the 17th century, but its second siege of Vienna in 1683 failed, and the Treaty of Karlowitz in 1699 forced major territorial losses for the first time. Over the following centuries the Ottomans steadily lost ground to Russia, Austria and rising nationalist movements in the Balkans, while struggling to reform their military and administration.

Reform efforts known as the Tanzimat (from 1839) sought to modernise law, the army and the state, and a short-lived constitution was granted in 1876. In 1908 the Young Turk Revolution restored constitutional rule, but successive wars — in Libya, the Balkans and beyond — stripped away most remaining European and African territories. By 1914 the much-diminished empire, often called 'the sick man of Europe', stood on the brink of a final, catastrophic war.

events.title: Reign of Süleyman the Magnificent
events.summary: Süleyman I presided over the Ottoman Empire at its territorial and cultural height, from Hungary to the Arab lands, codifying law and patronising the architecture of Sinan.

events.title: First Siege of Vienna
events.summary: Ottoman armies under Süleyman besieged Vienna, marking the high-water mark of Ottoman expansion into central Europe before withdrawing.

events.title: Treaty of Karlowitz
events.summary: After the failed 1683 siege of Vienna, the Treaty of Karlowitz ceded Hungary and other lands to the Habsburgs, the first major Ottoman territorial retreat in Europe.

events.title: The Tanzimat reforms
events.summary: A wave of modernising reforms reorganised Ottoman law, taxation, education and the military in an effort to halt the empire's decline and integrate its diverse subjects.

events.title: Young Turk Revolution
events.summary: The Young Turk movement forced Sultan Abdülhamid II to restore the constitution, ushering in a turbulent constitutional era dominated by the Committee of Union and Progress.

figures.name: Süleyman I ('the Magnificent')
figures.blurb: Ruled the Ottoman Empire at its zenith, expanding into Hungary and the Arab world, reforming its laws as 'the Lawgiver', and presiding over a golden age of Ottoman art.

figures.name: Mimar Sinan
figures.blurb: The greatest Ottoman architect, who designed hundreds of buildings including the Süleymaniye and Selimiye mosques, defining the classical Ottoman skyline.

pullquote.text: Süleyman the Magnificent ruled an empire of some 25 million people, from the gates of Vienna to the deserts of Arabia.

eras.title: World War I, the Armenian Genocide and the War of Independence
eras.standfirst: The empire's entry into the First World War brought the Armenian Genocide, defeat and partition — and then a national resistance that forged the republic.
body: The Ottoman Empire entered the First World War in late 1914 on the side of Germany and Austria-Hungary. Its forces repelled the Allied landings at Gallipoli in 1915–16, where a young officer named Mustafa Kemal distinguished himself, but the war elsewhere went badly, with defeats in the Caucasus, Mesopotamia and the Arab provinces.

Against this backdrop, in 1915–16 the ruling Committee of Union and Progress carried out the systematic deportation and mass killing of the empire's Armenian population. Beginning with the arrest of Armenian leaders in Constantinople on 24 April 1915, the regime drove Armenians on death marches into the Syrian desert amid massacres, starvation and exposure. Most historians regard these events as genocide — the first genocide of the 20th century — with estimates of the dead generally ranging from around 600,000 to as many as 1.5 million.

Ottoman defeat in 1918 was followed by Allied occupation and the punitive Treaty of Sèvres (1920), which would have dismembered Anatolia. Rejecting the treaty, Mustafa Kemal organised a nationalist resistance and a rival Grand National Assembly in Ankara in 1920. The ensuing War of Independence (1919–1922) defeated Greek, Armenian and French forces and expelled the occupiers.

Victory was sealed by the Treaty of Lausanne on 24 July 1923, which recognised the sovereignty of the new Turkish state within roughly its modern borders. The sultanate had already been abolished in November 1922, ending more than six centuries of Ottoman rule and clearing the way for a republic.

events.title: The Armenian Genocide
events.summary: From 24 April 1915 the Ottoman government deported and massacred its Armenian population in death marches and killings across Anatolia and into the Syrian desert; most historians recognise these events as genocide, with deaths estimated from roughly 600,000 to 1.5 million.

events.title: Gallipoli campaign
events.summary: Ottoman forces repelled an Allied attempt to seize the Dardanelles; the defence brought the officer Mustafa Kemal to national prominence.

events.title: Treaty of Sèvres
events.summary: The Allied treaty with the defeated Ottoman government proposed partitioning Anatolia among Greece, Armenia, Italy, France and others; rejected by the nationalists, it triggered the War of Independence.

events.title: Turkish War of Independence
events.summary: Mustafa Kemal led a national resistance and a new Grand National Assembly in Ankara, defeating Greek, Armenian and French forces and ending the Allied occupation of Anatolia.

events.title: Treaty of Lausanne
events.summary: Replacing the Treaty of Sèvres, the Lausanne treaty secured international recognition of Turkey's sovereignty and borders, the legal foundation of the modern state.

figures.name: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk
figures.role: Leader of the national movement; first president of Turkey
figures.blurb: Rose to fame at Gallipoli, then led the War of Independence and the Grand National Assembly, founding the Republic of Turkey in 1923 and serving as its first president and chief reformer.

pullquote.text: Most historians have concluded that the deportations and massacres of 1915–16 meet the definition of genocide — the intentional destruction of an ethnic or religious group.

eras.title: The Republic and Modern Türkiye
eras.standfirst: Atatürk's secular republic modernised radically, moved to multiparty democracy, joined NATO, and became a pivotal regional power.
body: On 29 October 1923 the Grand National Assembly proclaimed the Republic of Turkey and elected Mustafa Kemal its first president. Over the following years Atatürk drove a sweeping programme of reform: the caliphate was abolished in 1924, religion was separated from the state, the legal system was secularised on European models, the Latin alphabet replaced the Arabic script in 1928, and women gained the vote in national elections by 1934. Ankara became the capital in place of Istanbul.

Atatürk died in 1938 and was succeeded by İsmet İnönü. After the Second World War, in which Turkey remained largely neutral, the country moved to multiparty politics: the 1950 election brought the opposition Democrat Party to power in the first peaceful transfer of government. Turkey joined NATO in 1952, anchoring it firmly in the Western alliance during the Cold War, though its politics were repeatedly interrupted by military coups in 1960, 1971 and 1980.

Turkey was recognised as a candidate for European Union membership in 1999, and the early 2000s brought rapid economic growth under Recep Tayyip Erdoğan and the AK Party. Constitutional changes approved by referendum in 2017 and enacted in 2018 transformed the parliamentary system into a powerful executive presidency. In June 2022 the country registered its official name at the United Nations as 'Türkiye'.

Modern Türkiye is a populous, strategically pivotal state bridging Europe and Asia, a member of NATO and the G20, custodian of an extraordinary archaeological heritage, and a country still debating the balance between secular and religious identity that has run through its republican century.

events.title: Abolition of the caliphate
events.summary: The Grand National Assembly abolished the Ottoman caliphate and separated religion from the state, a cornerstone of Atatürk's secular reforms; a new constitution followed in April 1924.

events.title: The alphabet and language reforms
events.summary: Turkey replaced the Arabic script with a new Latin-based Turkish alphabet in 1928, part of reforms that sharply raised literacy and reoriented the nation toward Europe.

events.title: First multiparty election
events.summary: The Democrat Party won a landslide in the 1950 election, ending nearly three decades of one-party rule and marking Turkey's first peaceful transfer of power.

events.title: Turkey joins NATO
events.summary: Turkey became a member of NATO in 1952, cementing its strategic alignment with the West throughout the Cold War.

events.title: Registered at the UN as 'Türkiye'
events.summary: At the government's request, the United Nations registered the country's official name as 'Türkiye' in June 2022, replacing 'Turkey' in international usage.

figures.name: İsmet İnönü
figures.blurb: Atatürk's chief lieutenant and successor, who led Turkey through the Second World War as a neutral and oversaw its transition to multiparty democracy in 1950.

pullquote.text: In a single republican generation, Türkiye abolished the caliphate, adopted the Latin alphabet, enfranchised women and reoriented itself toward Europe.

figures.name: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk
figures.role: Founder and first president of the Republic of Turkey
figures.blurb: Soldier and statesman who led the Turkish War of Independence, founded the republic in 1923, and remade Turkey as a secular, modern nation-state through far-reaching reforms.

figures.name: Mehmed II ('the Conqueror')
figures.blurb: Conquered Constantinople in 1453, ending the Byzantine Empire and establishing the Ottoman imperial capital that would rule for centuries.

figures.name: Süleyman I ('the Magnificent')
figures.blurb: Presided over the Ottoman Empire at its height, expanding its frontiers and codifying its laws during a golden age of art and architecture.
